observations.recommendation:
We recommended and also re-iterated paraphrased audit recommendations of the prior years that the Local Chief Executive:
Require the Inventory Team to prepare a complete inventory report of PPEs, including the real properties, and have it reconciled with the GSO records. Thereafter, furnish the Audit Team a copy of the report;
Direct the GSO and the Accountant to continue updating its stock/property cards and ledger cards especially those acquired before 2017 to facilitate the reconciliation of property records and to immediately address any discrepancy found; and
Instruct the concerned personnel to work on securing the land titles of the remaining 124 lots and appropriate funds to cover its cost.

Management Comment:
According to the OIC-GSO, complete physical inventory taking have already been started, reports shall be submitted to the Audit Team as soon as possible. Problem with the absence of documents for the properties acquired from year 2015 and earlier was likewise mentioned. The City Accountant added that it is difficult to reconcile accounting records with the physical inventory due to the inadequacy of supporting documents.
Auditor’s Rejoinder:
Discussed and agreed during the exit conference that a physical inventory for each department shall be conducted for all existing/present properties that shall be used as basis for reconciliation with accounting records. The Audit Team thus cited the following supplementary audit recommendations:
We recommended that the Inventory Committee, offices of the GSO and Accounting shall exert extra effort to locate documents that could shed light to the unreconciled balances between records and investigate causes/reasons for missing or unaccounted properties, including those not found on site and identify the persons deemed liable thereof.
We further recommended that Management compel accountable officers/employees to submit required documents needed in the evaluation of request for relief from accountability for losses due to fortuitous events or natural calamities, or due to acts of man, i.e. theft, robbery, arson, etc.

We recommended and also re-iterated paraphrased audit recommendations of the prior years that the Local Chief Executive:
Require the Inventory Team to prepare a complete inventory report of PPEs, including the real properties, and have it reconciled with the GSO records. Thereafter, furnish the Audit Team a copy of the report;
Direct the GSO and the Accountant to continue updating its stock/property cards and ledger cards especially those acquired before 2017 to facilitate the reconciliation of property records and to immediately address any discrepancy found; and
Instruct the concerned personnel to work on securing the land titles of the remaining 124 lots and appropriate funds to cover its cost.

Management Comment:
According to the OIC-GSO, complete physical inventory taking have already been started, reports shall be submitted to the Audit Team as soon as possible. Problem with the absence of documents for the properties acquired from year 2015 and earlier was likewise mentioned. The City Accountant added that it is difficult to reconcile accounting records with the physical inventory due to the inadequacy of supporting documents.
Auditor’s Rejoinder:
Discussed and agreed during the exit conference that a physical inventory for each department shall be conducted for all existing/present properties that shall be used as basis for reconciliation with accounting records. The Audit Team thus cited the following supplementary audit recommendations:
We recommended that the Inventory Committee, offices of the GSO and Accounting shall exert extra effort to locate documents that could shed light to the unreconciled balances between records and investigate causes/reasons for missing or unaccounted properties, including those not found on site and identify the persons deemed liable thereof.
We further recommended that Management compel accountable officers/employees to submit required documents needed in the evaluation of request for relief from accountability for losses due to fortuitous events or natural calamities, or due to acts of man, i.e. theft, robbery, arson, etc.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require:
The City Treasurer to furnish the City Accountant a duly certified list showing the name of the taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year that shall be the basis of the latter to set-up RPT/SET Receivables at the beginning of the accounting period to conform with Sections 19 and 20, Volume 1, of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs;
The Assessor’s Office to expedite the updating of their data, specially the list of persons/entities who are exempted from payment of RPT (for dropping or dropped from assessment roll) to enable the Treasurer’s Office to update the tax information system of the LGU as required under Section 470, Article II, Title V of the Local Government Code of 1991; and
The City Treasurer to immediately post a notice of delinquency at the main hall and in publicly accessible and conspicuous place in each barangay in consonance with Section 254 of the Local Government Code of 1991 and also issue demand letter to the delinquent  taxpayers.  Such notice may also be posted in the City’s website.

Management Comment:
The OIC-City Assessor commented that the assessment roll includes both taxable and exempt and that the latter cannot be detached from the roll. Thus, the City Treasurer proposed to coordinate with the Enhanced Tax Revenue and Collection System (ETRACS) Administrator for the exclusion of exempt items, no commitment, however, as to when they will be able to come up with the duly certified list was made.

We recommended that the Accounting Unit conduct a thorough review and analysis of the details of the payable accounts and to submit the necessary documents to support the reversal entries made amounting to ₱27.68 million. Henceforth, they shall see to it that transactions shall be completely and appropriately supported before any journal entry is made.
Likewise, we recommended that the Accounting Unit exert extra effort in identifying payees who were deducted with Pag-IBIG Fund contribution but were not remitted to the Fund and refund the amount to the persons or remit the amount to the Fund, whichever is applicable.

Management Comment:
The Accounting Unit shall exert extra effort to locate and reconcile documents, majority of which are prior period transactions that shall support the reversal of accounts payable.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that Management direct the City Treasurer to coordinate with the City Accountant for the immediate reconciliation of the individual balances of the Cash in Local Treasury and Cash in Bank accounts between the GL and the Cashbooks for all funds and make the necessary adjustments thereon. Henceforth, in compliance with Section 189 of the GAAM, the City Treasurer and the City Accountant shall reconcile their books of accounts at least quarterly to avoid incurrence of accumulated unreconciled balances.
Further, we recommended that the City Treasurer/Accountable Officer regularly maintain and update complete cashbook per bank account and likewise maintain duly certified printed copy of the monthly updated and reconciled cashbook on file.

Management Comment:
The City Treasurer responded that they comply with the daily recording of transactions in their respective cashbooks. Reconciliation with the accounting records is being done upon the receipt of copies of the bank statements and the bank reconciliation statement prepared by Accounting Unit. The City Treasurer likewise mentioned that their office was unable to receive reconciling reports/documents from the Accounting Office for years 2016 and earlier. The City Treasurer added to have noted the audit recommendation the that their Office shall maintain and update complete cashbook per bank account and duly certified printed copy of the monthly updated and reconciled cashbook on file.
The City Accountant guaranteed that the unreconciled cash balances between the Accounting and the Treasurer’s records shall be investigated using available data and that the Accounting Office shall provide the City Treasurer with copies of the bank reconciliation statements and its supporting documents.

We recommended that the City Accountant:
Send demand letters to concerned officers and employees with unliquidated cash advances requesting immediate settlement thereof;
Withhold the salaries due the officers and employees should they fail to settle their cash advances within 15 days upon receipt of the demand letter;
Refrain from granting cash advances to an accountable officer unless his previous cash advance was already settled; and
Require the accountable officers to fully liquidate and settle their cash advances, specifically at the end of the year.
We also recommended that Management discontinue the practice of granting cash advance for travel to one official or employee and accommodate the travel cash advances of other officials and employees therein and to make certain that the cash advance check is drawn in the name of/issued to the specific concerned official/employee authorized to go on official travel.

Management Comment:
Demand letters are regularly sent to concerned officers/employees, according to the City Accountant. Grant of travel allowances to casual and job orders in the name of a certain LGU officer/employee was authorized/approved by the LCE.
Auditor’s Rejoinder:
Existing rules and regulations and rates of allowances for official local and foreign travels are prescribed for government personnel:
SECTION 1. COVERAGE.-
“This Executive Order shall govern all official local and foreign travels of government personnel both national and local. including personnel of government owned and/or controlled corporations and government financial institutions.” (E.O. No. 248)
“This Order shall cover official local and foreign travels of personnel of National Government Agencies (NGAs), including State Universities and Colleges (SUCs), Government Owned and Controlled Corporations (GOCCs), Government Financial Institutions (GFIs), Congress of the Philippines, Judiciary, Constitutional Commissions, Office of the Ombudsman and the Local Government Units (LGUs).” (E. O No. 77)

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) instruct the Accounting Unit to completely account for the recorded Due to NMIS amounting to ₱460,236 to correctly identify the amount for remittance to NMIS considering the change of City Slaughterhouse from class “AA” to LRME. Once properly identified, the City Treasurer shall immediately remit the amount due to the NMIS.
Further, we recommended that the Accounting Unit provide individual subsidiary ledgers for the account Due to NGAs for an efficient recording of all transactions and outright identification of individual balances.

Management Comment:
The Office of the City Treasurer will immediately remit the amount due to NMIS once the Accounting Office have completely accounted for the amount due to the said national government agency.
The Accounting Office promised to verify the subsidiary ledgers and supporting documents to completely account for the amount that shall be remitted if ever to NMIS.

We recommended that Management, in the subsequent receipt of cash and in-kind donations, direct:
The Treasurer and the Accountant to regularly provide the Budget Officer necessary data/reports prescribed under Section IV-B, Item 6 of COA Circular No. 2014-002, for the latter to be able to prepare the Registry of Cash Donations and Utilization for all cash donations received by the City Government for DRRM programs and the Reports on the Receipt and Distribution/Utilization/Issuance of cash donations;
The Accounting Office to prepare Subsidiary Ledgers for cash donations received in accordance with Section IV-B, Item 7 of COA Circular No. 2014-002; and
The General Services/Property and Supply Officer to prepare the Registry of Donated Relief Goods for DRRM, monthly reports on the receipt and distribution/utilization/issuance of in-kind donations and prepare/maintain an inventory list of all donated items.

Management Comment:
The audit recommendations were noted by the concerned officers and employees of the City.

We recommended that Management instruct the Accountant to submit immediately the ORs and VDS for the months of October to December 2018 and the accounts for the months of July to December 2018, including the corresponding Financial Reports and henceforth strictly comply with the timelines on the recording of accounts and submission of accounts and reports to the Auditor, within the first ten (10) days of the ensuing month, as required by existing laws, rules and regulations.

Management Comment:
During the exit conference, the City Accountant commented that as of date, all accounts and financial reports are 100 percent submitted to the Office of the Auditor. The City Accountant likewise explained that delay in the processing and submission of accounts is mainly due to the late submission of the disbursement vouchers by each Department. In addition, documents submitted to Accounting Unit for processing are incomplete or there are deficiencies in the required supporting documents. The Accounting Unit has to wait for the documents on all disbursements chargeable to the current year, though items/services has not been served for the said year. It has been the practice of the Accounting Unit to record/set-up Accounts Payable for these transactions before the year ends. He added that all the Department Heads were already informed through a letter regarding the timely submission of DVs before the end of the calendar year.
Auditor’s Rejoinder
The practice of recording Accounts Payables for items not yet delivered or services not yet rendered should be stopped. Only those disbursements that were chargeable to the 2018 fund with purchase order/approved contract shall be obligated.
We recommended that Management come up with an updated procedure/system on the submission of documents by each Department on the procurement of goods and services during the last quarter of the calendar year and for the Accounting Unit to stop the practice of recording as accounts payable those goods not yet delivered and services not yet rendered. In such way, delay in the submission of accounts and preparation of financial reports will be minimized.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that Management:
Direct the Accountant to expedite the submission of the Year-end Trial Balance and financial statements. Henceforth, prepare and submit Monthly Trial Balances and Quarterly Financial Statements with complete schedules and annexes within the prescribed timeframe in accordance with the abovementioned existing rules and regulations;
Take appropriate immediate remedial measures, such as augmenting the Accountant’s Office of personnel from other Departments, especially during report preparations and/or allowing them to render overtime, subject to the availability of funds, in order to comply with the timely preparation and submission of these mandatory financial reports, pursuant to mandated deadlines; and
Allow the Accountant to recommend qualified plantilla personnel of the Accounting Office to attend Trainings or Seminars relative to their works, to equip them with the required knowledge and skills in order to continue improving their performance.

Management Comment:
The City Accountant mentioned that delay in the preparation of financial reports was likewise caused by the late processing and submission of documents by each Department which processes separately or by office the procurement of office supplies, goods, others.
Auditor’s Rejoinder:
Management was reminded of CY 2017 audit recommendation that they shall require all concerned to streamline the procurement/acquisition process in order to expedite the conduct of procurement activities of the City. Further, to strictly observe the applicable rules and regulations on the procurement, receipt, acceptance and issuance of goods in compliance with the 2016 IRR of R.A. No. 9184 and the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs. Said audit recommendation, however, according to the OIC-GSO was not yet implemented in 2018.

We recommended that:
The Treasurer’s Office coordinate with the Accounting Unit on the use of account titles, grouping of account titles or sub-titles, particularly on Income Accounts, for efficiency of recording and preparation of collection reports in accordance with PPSAS:
Management indicate the projected amount on all expected revenues for the year, specifically in budget preparation in order to provide assurance in funding of plans and programs; and
Require the City Treasurer to revisit the collection accomplishment reports quarterly to come up with other possible measures in improving the City’s revenue collections as well as for efficient reporting.

Management Comment:
The City Treasurer promised to coordinate with the ETRACS Administrator the adjustment of the of account titles, grouping of account titles or sub-titles of the income accounts.
Also discussed during the Exit Conference, since the enactment of the Tax Revenue Code of 1997, the Sangguniang Pnalungsod has not passed a resolution adjusting the tax rates although it was given the authority by the Local Government Code to adjust rates of tax ordinances not oftener than once every five years.
Auditor’s Rejoinder:
We recommended that the Sangguniang Panlungsod revisit/review the existing Local Revenue Code of the City of Tabaco enacted in 1997.
We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Treasurer, Municipal Assessor and other concerned Department Heads to prepare and submit to the Sangguniang Panlungsod for approval a proposed adjustment on tax rates to fully tap the scope of the LGU's taxing power, pursuant to Section 191 of R.A. No. 7160, in order to increase tax revenues and receipts through updating of the rates in the existing tax code, which will augment the budget provision for a better delivery of government services.

We recommended and re-iterated paraphrased prior year’s audit recommendation that Management require:
All concerned officials and employees to strictly enforce payment of current and past due rentals totaling at least to ₱17.54 million and fully impose the provisions of the City Ordinance and the Contract of Lease particularly when there is breach of contract;
The Market Committee to formulate or develop effective and practical strategies that could enhance collections of all unpaid accounts and effect imposition of penalties/surcharges to defaulting lessees as provided under the terms and conditions of the lease contract to compel prompt rental payment;
The City Treasurer to maintain a complete and updated individual subsidiary ledgers of all stallholders in coordination with the Market Administrator/ OIC-Economic Enterprise Management Unit and other offices responsible over the grant/award of stalls to new stallholders. Henceforth, submit the complete and updated schedule/report of all accounts with the City Accounting Office for recording;
The City Treasurer to develop adequate monitoring system for an easy and prompt access of information on the status of market stallholders’ account to avoid the grant of prompt discount to delinquent stallholders; and
The Market Committee to formulate plans and adopt strategies/measures to maximize utilization of the spaces or stalls with the end view of generating additional income.

Management Comment:
The Office of the City Treasurer is currently in the process of integrating information and communication technology in the management of City market collection. They are in fact in the database build up identifying the stalls and updating the records which is a component of the ETRACS. The Office believe that with the ongoing innovations, collections will be intensified and ledgers both in electronic and hard copy forms will be completed and updated.
The City Treasurer proposed that Management provide her Office additional IT literate personnel who shall be handling the records of the market operation. She also agreed during the exit conference that the compromise agreement are not religiously complied with. Furthermore, the City Treasurer gave assurance that the following shall be acted upon:
Correction/Adjustment of erroneosly granted prompt payment discount
Submission of the complete and updated subsidiary ledgers

We recommended that Management apply feasible strategies to enforce the collection of rent on a yearly basis from relatives of occupied cemetery lots for more than five years.

Management Comment:
The City Treasurer is amenable to the audit findings and recommendations and promised to coordinate with the concerned officers/employees for the proper implementation of policies.

We recommended that Management require the LSB to formulate/prepare the required three-year strategic program and the recommendation of the DepEd PSDS and SDS as well as the required SIPs and other information from DepEd RO, which are supposedly the bases for the preparation and approval by the LSB of the 2018 SEF Annual Budget and also furnish the Audit Team of the same for reference and review purposes. Otherwise, non-compliance thereof may result in Notices of Suspension of the transactions incurred without the afore-cited required documents for the preparation and approval by LSB of the 2018 SEF Annual Budget.
Henceforth, we recommended that Management require the LSB to strictly follow the mandated required processes and documents in planning and budgeting for SEF under the revised guidelines on the use of SEF, pursuant to the above-mentioned Joint Circular to avoid Notices of Suspensions or Disallowances. Without such required documents, the LSB has no basis to approve the SEF Annual Budget.

Management Comment:
The representative of the School Division Superentendent gave assurance on the submission of SIPS.

We recommended that the SEF Local School Board prepare the Income and Expenditure Estimates for the current year using the formula provided under DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017 dated January 19, 2017, to come up with reasonable estimates for the priority needs of public schools within the City.

Management Comment:
During the exit conference, the SDS representative commented that the audit findings and recommendations are well taken and that these shall be referred to the SDS and other concerned officers of the Board.

We recommended that Management require the LSB to prepare and submit the required quarterly and annual reports to the Oversight Department/Agency in the prescribed form and within the prescribed period and observe the posting of Utilization Report in the Agency/LGU website to facilitate effective monitoring of the allocation and utilization of the SEF thereby, promoting transparency and accountability.

Management Comment:
Management concurred with the audit recommendations and that posting of required report to the City’s official website shall be made in the promotion of government transparency and accountability.

We recommended that the Local School Board/concerned officials, also give attention and priority to:  a) operation and maintenance of public schools; b) construction and repair of school building, facilities and equipment; and c) ECCD Program. Further, to strictly adhere to the provisions of R.A. No. 7160 and DepEd-DBM and DILG Joint Circular No. 01 series of 2017 in the usage of the SEF. Furthermore, we recommended for the Local School Board to judiciously identify priority programs and projects for the coming year.

Management Comment:
During the exit conference, the SDS representative commented that the audit findings and recommendations are well taken and that these shall be referred to the SDS and other concerned officers of the Board.

We however re-iterated our prior year’s recommendation that Management continue to reinforce the involvement and participation of barangays through massive information dissemination of the City’s solid waste management programs especially on the proper disposal of waste. Also, to strictly monitor areas wherein discriminate dumping of waste is rampant and enforce all regulations to the extent of imposing penalties for non-compliance.

We commended the City for its initial compliance on the Post Closure and Rehabilitation of Closed Dumpsite Project at Barangay San Antonio, Tabaco City. We however recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the concerned officials and employees to expedite the implementation of required activities in accordance with the EMB recommendations and guidelines set in R.A. No. 9003.

Management Comment:
Management committed to implement the Post Closure and Rehabilitation projects in accordance with the EMB recommendations and guidelines set in R.A. No. 9003.

We recommended that Management, through the GAD Focal Person, spearhead the setting up and maintenance of the GAD database to serve as basis for gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation to be able to undertake thorough gender analysis based on the relevant data for proper identification of existing gender issues and ensure that approved PPAs to be incorporated in the GPB are gender-responsive.  Likewise, use HGDG, PIMME and FIMME forms as tools in identifying programs and projects attributed to GAD budget for efficiency in identifying the issues, analysis, and monitoring its effectiveness.

Management Comment:
The GAD Focal Point System Chairperson considered the recommendation and will request and formulate a team on setting up and maintenance of the GAD database to serve as basis for gender responsive planning, programming and policy formulation to be able to undertake thorough gender analysis based on the relevant data for proper indication of existing gender issues and ensure that the approved PPAs to be incorporated  in the GPB are gender-responsive. The GAD Office-Tabaco in coordination with the Provincial GAD Office had previously implemented trainings and provided HGDG tools to be used in identifying programs and projects attributed to GAD budget for efficiency in identifying the issues, analysis and in monitoring its effectiveness.

We recommended that Management consider judicious use,  the efficient and effective actions in the disbursement of government fund in addressing gender issues towards the realization of the objectives of City’s commitments, plans and policies on women empowerment and gender equality.
Further, we recommended strict compliance with Section 3.2 of DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 02 dated September 23, 2016.
Furthermore, we recommended that Management remind the concerned officials and employees on the guidelines for the grant of cash advances that only duly appointed or designated disbursing officers may perform disbursing functions.

Management Comment:
The main objective of the travel is to visit successful Teen Centers in Marikina and Iloilo. The GAD-concerned officers/employees have noted the audit findings and recommendations.

We recommended that Management strictly adhere to the guidelines on the preparation of the GAD Accomplishment Report provided in the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circulars 2016-01 and 2013-01 and on the on the timely submission of the GAD Plan and Budget and Accomplishment Report to COA Audit Team as prescribed in Item V of COA Circular No. 2014-001.

Management Comment:
The GAD Plan and Budget and Accomplishment Report were not submitted within the prescribed period due to some unreconciled items. The GFPS Chairperson and GAD personnel sincerely apologize for the delay and gave assurance that AR shall be prepared quarterly to make sure that they could fully submit on time in the succeeding reporting period.

We recommended that Management revisit their programs/projects, giving more attention and study on how to maximize the utilization of the GAD funds through the implementation of GAD related programs and projects in order to attain the objectives for which the funds were integrated.
We also reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendation that Management allocate at least five per cent of the total budget for the implementation of GAD-related activities as required under Section 30 of the General Appropriations Act and Section 36 (a) of R.A. 9710 (Magna Carta of Women).

Management Comment:
The GFPS Chairperson submitted to the audit recommendations that all programs and projects shall be geared towards localization of the Magna Carta of Women and other pertinent laws for the empowerment of women.

We recommended that Management facilitate the full implementation of the BUB and LGSF-ADM programs/projects/activities in order not to delay the benefits these could provide the intended beneficiaries.

Management Comment:
According to the BUB Focal Person, as of March 2019, implementation of BUB and Yaman Pinoy projects is almost complete as remaining fund amounted to only ₱411,645.25 and ₱188,257.05 respectively.

We reiterated prior year’s recommendation that the City Government, through the CDRRMO ensure efficient and effective execution of projects designed to avoid, lessen or limit the potential adverse impact of disasters, such as those projects which pertain to disaster prevention/mitigation and disaster preparedness.

Management Comment:
For the current appropriation for CY 2018, the Construction of Permanent Evacuation Center worth ₱4.00 million was not realized because acquisition of the lot in Brgy. Tabiguian where said evacuation center is to be constructed is still in process. The construction of permanent comfort rooms at different evacuation centers was cancelled because according to the IRR of RA 10821, the school shall only be the last recourse for evacuation, hence, the CDRRMC decided that these projects be no longer implemented in school campuses. With regard to the the fabrication of mobile CR, the City Health Officer suggested that a septic tank shall be constructed first before a mobile CR is installed. These projects shall be implemented once the other requirements/documents become available.
For the re-programmed unutilized LDRRMF for CYs 2011 – 2015, per CDRRMC Res. No. 004-2016, the total amount re-programmed was ₱33.43 million, however, ₱1.17 million is still not yet submitted for bidding for the following reasons:
a.    The amount is not sufficient for the rehabilitation of water tank reservoir; and
b.    The City has not yet found a supplier for fiber glass boat.
These projects, however, will be implemented once the requirements become available.
Difficulty in finding a supplier for the procurement of mobile water purifier and a drone and the delayed quotation from suppliers on the procurement of dump truck hindered the utilization of re-programmed unutilized LDRRMF for CYs 2014-2017.

We commended Management for extending a 24-hour ambulance service to its constituents and the general public.  We recommend that Management continue its aim in upholding an efficient and effective delivery of health services and making health one of its priority programs.

Management Comment:
The LDRRM Officer expressed gratitude for the commendation given to the Communication Development Unit for a job well-done and gave assurance that the Unit shall continue to uphold an efficient and effective delivery of service to the people in the best way they can.

We recommended that Management meticulously and judiciously review and evaluate projects before its inclusion in the Local Development Fund and Annual Investment Plan.
Further, Management to observe proper timing in the implementation of the infrastructure projects to give ample time for the implementing office in the prompt accomplishment of the projects.
Furthermore, we recommended that Management closely monitor and ensure the efficient implementation of all priority development projects and programs under the 20% DF that are envisioned to help in the realization of social-economic and environmental benefits as prescribed under DILG and DBM Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2017-01 dated 22 February 2017.
We also recommended that Management ensure the re-appropraition for eligible development projects of the prior years’ savings from the implementation of projects under the 20% DF for its optimum utilization and attainment of desirable developments in the City.

Management Comment:
Management commented that delay in the implementation of programs and projects was mainly due to incomplete supporting documents and the late information on the inadequacy of supporting documents. It was also discussed during the exit conference that during budgeting, programs and projects shall be fully supported with the plans and specifications, program of works, and others necessary documents.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the duly constituted Inventory Committee/Team to conduct a thorough and complete physical inventory of the Agency’s properties in accordance with Sections 490 and 491 of GAAM Volume I, prepare and submit the RPCPPE in compliance with Section 124 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume 1.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the City Accountant to: (a) strictly monitor and conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis and validation of the existence of the receivable and payable accounts; (b) prepare necessary adjusting entries for transactions affecting the accounts; (c) prepare detailed aging of receivables and payables once valid supporting documents are retrieved; (d) request for write-off of uncollectible receivables from the books of accounts to the Commission on Audit supported by documents required by the Circular; and (e) issue demand letters or bill of accounts to those identified debtors.

We recommended that Management:

Require City Treasurer furnish the City Accountant, on a periodic basis the necessary data/reports/documents regarding market stall rentals for recording purposes; and

Require the City Accountant that the total realizable income from market stall rental fees is properly taken up in the books of the City Government.

We recommended that the City Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting entry to correct the over and understatement in the accounts Advances to Operating Expenses and Due from LGUs amounting to ₱269,160 and ₱200,000 respectively.

We recommended that Management:

Require the City Accountant to check the correctness of the reported payable accounts  particularly those beginning balances supported with valid documents and thereafter initiate its immediate payments/remittances to all concerned barangay; and

Require the City Treasurer to remit to the concerned barangays all amount due them as stipulated in Section 271 (d) of The Local Government Code.

We recommended that Management direct the City Treasurer and the City Accountant to coordinate for the immediate reconciliation of the individual balances of the Cash account balances for all funds as of December 31, 2017 and to promptly record all necessary adjusting entries. 

Henceforth, in compliance with Section 189 of the GAAM, the City Treasurer and the City Accountant shall reconcile their books of accounts at least quarterly to avoid incurrence of accumulated unreconciled balances.

We recommended that the LCE require the CSWD Officer to update regularly their records more particularly those data needed in the preparation of payroll for the Longevity Gift and the Social Pension. 

The Barangay Social Workers and the BASCA Presidents shall be asked to pool resources and be responsible for updating the records/reports relative to the status of the senior citizens of their barangay and regularly submit report to the CSWD.

Pay-out of quarterly social pension shall be on the first month of every quarter in compliance with DSWD Memorandum Circular No. 17 Series of 2015

We also recommended vigilance by the Accounting Unit in processing cash advances to guarantee completeness and properly supported transactions to minimize occurrence of recurrent excess in the cash advances.

We recommended that the LCE require the City Accountant to maintain Subsidiary Ledgers and schedules for Cash Advances to Disbursing Officers/Special Disbursing Officers to facilitate monitoring and reconciliation with the Treasury Unit.

We recommended that Management require strict compliance to prescribed rules and procedures on the submission by the contractor of a Construction Safety and Health Program duly approved by DOLE which shall form part of the contract.

Also, for the Head of the Agency to ensure the strict compliance to Section 3.1 of the COA Circular No. 2013-004 by requiring the concerned department/offices/units to appropriately inform the SA or ATL of the notification to the public relative to the its PPAs.

We recommended that Management require all concerned to streamline the procurement/acquisition process in order to expedite the conduct of procurement activities of the City. Further, to strictly observe the applicable rules and regulations on the procurement, receipt, acceptance and issuance of goods in compliance with the 2016 IRR of RA No. 9184 and the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs.

We recommended that Management:

Direct the Accountant to expedite the submission of the Year-end Trial Balance and financial statements. Henceforth, prepare and submit Monthly Trial Balances and Quarterly Financial Statements with complete schedules and annexes within the prescribed timeframe in accordance with the abovementioned existing rules and regulations;

Take appropriate immediate remedial measures, such as augmenting the Accountant’s Office of personnel from other Departments, especially during report preparations and/or allowing them to render overtime, subject to the availability of funds, in order to comply with the timely preparation and submission of these mandatory financial reports, pursuant to mandated deadlines; and

Allow the Accountant to recommend qualified plantilla personnel of the Accounting Office to attend Trainings or Seminars relative to their works, to equip them with the required knowledge and skills in order to continue improving their performance.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management require the Local School Board and the other concerned officials to strictly adhere to the provisions of R.A. No. 7160 and DepEd-DBM and DILG Joint Circular No. 01 series of 2017 in the usage of the SEF, otherwise this warrant the issuance of audit disallowances.

We also reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation enjoining Management to direct  the City Accountant to refrain from processing payment of those expenses that are not legally and appropriately chargeable against the Special Education Fund, to avoid repeated disallowances in audit.

We recommended that the Management: (a) reinforce the involvement and participation of barangays through massive information dissemination of the City’s solid waste management programs especially on the proper disposal of waste; and (b) support the establishment of fully functional MRF for each barangay or cluster barangay and in the meantime, provide appropriate trash bins with proper labels on assigned collection areas.

Further, we recommended the strict monitoring of areas where: (a) indiscriminate dumping of waste is rampant; and (b) burning of waste is being practiced and enforce all regulations to the extent of imposing penalties for non-compliance.

	Furthermore, we recommended that the City hasten the construction and proper utilization of the City MRF, complete rehabilitation of the closed dump site at Barangay San Antonio and to fully implement all the solid waste management projects, programs and activities to ensure the protection of public health and environment.
We recommended that Management facilitate the full implementation of all BuB programs/projects/activities in order not to delay further the benefits these could provide the intended beneficiaries.

We recommended that Management:

Direct the City Treasurer and OIC-Economic Enterprise Management Unit to strictly enforce collection for the unpaid rentals and strictly impose the provisions of the aforementioned City Ordinance and the Contract of Lease particularly when there is breach of contract; and

Formulate plans and adopt strategies/measures to maximize utilization of the spaces or stalls with the end view of generating additional income.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that Management ensure: 

That the remaining projects not implemented in CY 2017 be completed in the succeeding year in order that the benefits thereof may be fully enjoyed by the targeted beneficiaries; and

The efficient and economical implementation of projects funded under the 20 percent Local Development Fund in order to achieve the social, economic and environmental development of the City.

Further, we recommended that the City Budget and City Accounting Office to conduct monthly reconciliation of their respective records.

We recommended to the Local Chief Executive (LCE) the following paraphrased prior year’s audit recommendations:

Instruct the City Accountant to  prepare and maintain PPELC and RPLC, starting with the current year’s acquisition to prior year’s acquisition and aim to increase the coverage until all PPE items are covered;

Require the City Treasurer and the City Accountant to reconcile inventory results with property records and those maintained at the City Accounting Office and isolate items not reconciled for easy reference in succeeding inventory taking. Aim to reduce the number and amount of unreconciled PPE account balances; and

Direct the City Treasurer to secure the documents to support the ownership of the real properties.

We also recommended that the LCE require the duly constituted Inventory Committee/Team to conduct a thorough and complete physical inventory of the agency’s properties in accordance with Section 490 and 491 of GAAM Volume I.

We also reiterated last year’s audit recommendation to require the City Accountant to exert extra effort in gathering supporting data/documents for them to analyze/verify the details of the reconciling items, prepare the necessary correcting/adjusting entries and record the same in the books in adherence to Section 3.3 of COA Circular No. 96-11 and The National Guidelines on Internal Control Systems.

We recommended that Management strengthen the control policies and procedures of cash collections by instructing the City Treasurer and Liquidating Officer to check and verify the Report of Collections and Deposits being submitted by Collectors to prevent errors and minimize wrongful acts, if not totally eliminated.

We also recommended that proper supervision be made by the City Treasurer to all her staff to help ensure that internal control objectives are achieved.

We likewise reiterated prior year’s audit recommendation on the preparation of Bank Reconciliation, that the City Accountant exert extra effort in gathering data/documents for them to identify/analyze/verify reconciling items, particularly those that are indicative of possible fraud.

Lastly, for the Management to continue the investigation of the uncovered cash discrepancy perpetrated by certain individual/s, and file a criminal and administrative case against those persons found liable in the said fraud.

We recommended that Management require the BAC to formulate/develop strategies to comply with the recommended earliest possible time allowed for the procurement of goods and infrastructure projects with the end view of ensuring an effective and efficient procurement process.

We recommended that Management require the concerned officials and employees to utilize the Fiesta Fund raised out of donations/contributions received from other LGUs, civic organizations and private individuals in disbursing/paying for the expenses of fiesta/festival celebration/activities, before authorizing the use of other funds, if any.
Require all the  sixteen payees who actually received the honoraria payments, to immediately refund the amount they received, to the Office of the City Treasurer for lack of valid and legal basis

We recommended that the Local School Board, the City Budget Officer and other concerned officials to strictly adhere to the provisions of R.A. No. 7160 and DECS, DBM and DILG Joint Circular No. 01 series of 1998 on the allocation and utilization of the SEF, judiciously identifying and giving equal consideration among the priority programs and projects that should be funded, in order that the objectives for which the fund was established shall be achieved.  

The City Accountant is likewise enjoined to refrain from processing payment of claims that are not legally and appropriately chargeable against the Special Education Fund, to avoid disallowances in audit.

We recommended the full implementation of programs/projects/activities for the immediate delivery of services to the constituents on the proper collection, transfer, recycling and composting of waste as mandated by law.

We also reiterated the following prior year’s audit recommendation: 

Strict implementation of the prescribed procedures and guidelines under R.A. No. 9003 on the solid waste segregation at source;

Conversion of the City’s dumpsite to an Ecological Park or Sanitary Landfill (SLF) in compliance with Section 37 of the Act; and

Intensify the SWM information dissemination campaign and enforce all regulations to the extent of imposing penalties for non-compliance.

We re-iterated our prior year’s recommendation that the City Government, through the CDRRMO ensure that the execution of projects designed to avoid, lessen or limit the potential adverse impact of disaster, such as those projects pertaining to disaster prevention/mitigation and disaster preparedness, shall be given utmost priority.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the OIC-General Service Officer should conduct an actual physical inventory of all properties and ensure that all donations received are properly accounted and recorded in the RPCPPE. Also, for the concerned Officials to furnish the deed of donation and any other documents to the Office of the OIC-GSO and the City Accounting Office for recording purposes. Further, an appraisal of the donated properties must be made by the City Appraisal Committee as basis for the recording.
We recommended that the City Mayor require the BAC to prepare and submit the Procurement Monitoring Report in the prescribed format as required under Section 12.2 of the IRR of R.A. No. 9184.

We recommended that the City Accountant immediately prepare and submit the Bank Reconciliation Statements for all its bank accounts for the month of December 2015 for the immediate preparation of the correcting/adjusting entries for discrepancies/errors or other reconciling items requiring corrections in order to reflect the correct balance of the cash account in the financial statements and henceforth, prepare and timely submit the monthly BRS to the Auditor.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive requires the Accountable Officers, City Treasurer and the City Accountant to strictly comply with the prescribed period for the submission of accounts pursuant to COA Circular Nos. 94-001 and 2009-006.

The City Accountant require the concerned suppliers to pay the liquidated damages totaling P30,149.14 for the late deliveries of  procured drugs and medicines and henceforth, strictly impose the penalty of 1/10th of 1 percent for each day of delay in the delivery of the goods purchased to encourage prompt delivery by the suppliers.

We recommended that based on the documents, the City Treasurer shall record the receipt and issuance in the Registry of Donated Relief Goods for DRRM.  Separate registries shall be maintained for each kind of donated goods.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following courses of action:

The BAC streamline its procurement process in order to expedite the conduct of public bidding of projects funded by the 20% Development Fund;

The implementing offices ensure that complete documentation for the proposed projects are submitted and regular follow-up with the concerned offices is made; and

The City Engineering Office ensure that all infrastructure projects planned during the year are implemented.

We recommended that the City Government gradually create regular plantilla positions in the City Accounting Office, subject to availability of funds, to ensure continuity of service by its personnel and to promote operational efficiency.

We recommended to OIC-City Accountant to prepare the bank reconciliation statements for all funds promptly at the end of each month.  A copy thereof shall be furnished to the Audit Team for review, together with the proposed Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs), if any, in case of adjustments.  She shall follow up with the LGU’s depository bank the monthly bank statements to ensure its timely release or secure a snapshot copy of the bank transactions at the end of the month to facilitate the preparation of the bank reconciliation statements.

We recommended that the local government shall make a supreme effort to dispose its unserviceable property and equipment in accordance with Section 125 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Vol. I, to obtain better re-sale value and/or prevent further deterioration of the assets due to long exposure to elements.

Observe promptness in the preparation and approval of procurement documents from Purchase Request to Request For Quotations until it reaches the Purchase Order to avoid unnecessary and improve process efficiency.
